title: Why NAS Is Expensive
concept: NAS can be expensive because many candidate architectures must be trained, estimated, or evaluated, which can consume large amounts of compute time, hardware, and energy.

exercises.prompt: Recap: why can a NAS search space grow quickly?

options.text: Because combining choices about blocks, widths, depths, and connections creates many candidates
Because every CNN must contain infinitely many layers
Because NAS ignores all boundaries and tests nothing

exercises.explain: Exactly — and once there are many candidates, evaluating them becomes the expensive part.

cards.title: Testing many runners costs more than choosing one
cards.body: If you want the fastest runner, one option is to hold a race with many people. That gives you evidence, but it also takes time and effort. NAS faces the same basic issue: comparing lots of model candidates usually means spending compute on lots of model candidates.

cards.title: Where the cost comes from
cards.body: NAS is expensive because candidate architectures often need to be trained enough to judge, then validated, and sometimes measured for latency or memory too. Even if each single model is affordable, repeating that process across many candidates can multiply the cost dramatically.

cards.title: Taste-testing hundreds of soup recipes
cards.body: Trying one soup recipe is manageable. Trying 300 slightly different versions means buying more ingredients, using more stove time, and washing more pots. NAS can feel similar: each candidate may be only a small variation, but the total bill grows because there are so many of them.

cards.title: Small costs add up fast
cards.body: Suppose a team trains 200 candidate CNNs for even a short evaluation run. If each candidate takes real GPU time plus validation and latency checks, the total cost can become huge. That is why NAS research often looks for shortcuts like weight sharing, smaller proxy tasks, or early stopping — but the core problem remains expensive comparison.

cards.title: Computer search is not 'free because it is automated'
cards.body: Automation reduces human labor, but it does not remove compute cost. A loop that launches 1,000 candidate trainings may save manual clicking, yet it can still burn a large amount of hardware time, electricity, and money.

cards.title: Why NAS bills can grow
cards.body: The pain is usually in repetition, not in one single choice.

left.text:
Train one model
Validate once
Manageable single cost

right.text:
Repeat training many times
Repeat validation many times
Total compute explodes

exercises.prompt: What is the main reason NAS can become expensive?

options.text: Because many candidate models may need training and evaluation
Because NAS requires printing every model on paper first
Because convolution layers cannot run on GPUs
Because labels become unreadable after search

exercises.explain: Right — the repeated evaluation of many candidates is the core cost driver.

exercises.prompt: True or false: if a NAS process is automated, its compute cost automatically becomes negligible.
exercises.explain: False — automation helps workflow, but compute and energy costs can still be large.

exercises.sentence: NAS often becomes costly because model evaluation is repeated across many ___ architectures.
exercises.explain: Exactly — repeated candidate testing is what drives the bill upward.

exercises.prompt: Which situation best explains why NAS can overwhelm a budget?

options.text: A search loop trains and validates hundreds of possible CNN designs
A designer draws one network diagram on a whiteboard
A student crops one image for a presentation slide

exercises.explain: Yes — scaling candidate evaluation across many models is the expensive part.

exercises.prompt: A company says, 'NAS sounds promising, but every extra candidate means more training time, more validation runs, and more GPU cost.' What important NAS lesson are they identifying?

options.text: NAS can be expensive because model comparison is repeated across many candidate architectures
NAS is free once one candidate is trained
NAS avoids evaluation entirely by definition
NAS cost comes mainly from drawing architecture diagrams

exercises.explain: Exactly. The cost comes from repeated search-time evaluation, not from the phrase 'search' itself. Next: if NAS is so automated, what decisions do humans still have to make?
